title: closure mutation not recorded in threadPaused, also assertion wrong
description:
While experimenting with making all black holes eager, I found that I sometimes saw segfaults, so I've been investigating. I think I found the problem.

My recent fix for eager-blackhole deadlocks, !14734, avoids overwriting eager black holes, to prevent losing wakeups. While it's correct to not overwrite the black hole contents, I found two problems with the fix, one serious:

- (serious): `recordClosureMutated` is not called on an eager black hole. While `ThreadPaused.c` does not mutate the closure, we must still call `recordClosureMutated` because the mutator changed it.
- (less serious): the line `ASSERT(blackHoleOwner(bh) == tso || blackHoleOwner(bh) == NULL);` is incorrect, since with `-feager-blackholing` we get non-atomic eager black holes. Multiple threads may race and claim. The usual mechanism for thunk updating resolves the collision.

title: ghc-9.15.20250912: typing plugins plus `-fpolymorphic-specialisation` cause SIGSEGV when the program is run
description:
## Summary

This may be related to #23109, since the code snapshot is from a similar date, so I've added it to the list in that ticket. I haven't tried most of the other repros from the list yet with current HEAD, but current master of horde-ad compiles and runs fine at least (which also means this is not blocking me personally in any way).

This test crashes at runtime. Running under gdb shows it's a SIGSEGV:

```
gdb dist-newstyle/build/x86_64-linux/ghc-9.15.20250912/horde-ad-0.1.0.0/t/simplifiedOnlyTest/build/simplifiedOnlyTest/simplifiedOnlyTest
...
Thread 5 "ghc_worker" received signal SIGSEGV, Segmentation fault.
[Switching to Thread 0x7fffe7fff6c0 (LWP 442542)]
0x0000000001bb6ff8 in stg_ap_0_fast ()
(gdb) bt
#0  0x0000000001bb6ff8 in stg_ap_0_fast ()
#1  0x0000000000000000 in ?? ()
```

Removing `--ghc-options="-fpolymorphic-specialisation"` from the repro makes the test pass.

It's possible the cause is unsafe operations in my code that happen to show their unsoundness only when more specialization happens, but I think it's not very likely.

I've minimized the code minimally so far, but I'm ready to assist in any way. Linting (`-dcore-lint -dstg-lint -dcmm-lint -dtag-inference-checks`) doesn't error out.

## Steps to reproduce

```
git clone git@github.com:Mikolaj/horde-ad.git
cd horde-ad
git checkout VTA-fail
rm -f cabal.project.local
rm -rf ~/.cabal/packages/head.hackage.ghc.haskell.org 
cabal update
cabal test -w /home/mikolaj/r/ghc.13092025/ghc/_build/stage1/bin/ghc --allow-newer --enable-optimization --ghc-options="-fpolymorphic-specialisation"
```

## Expected behavior

Test passes with `--ghc-options="-fpolymorphic-specialisation"`.

## Environment

* GHC version used: ghc-9.15.20250912

Optional:

* Operating System: Ubuntu
* System Architecture: AMD64

title: naturalAndNot truncates result when the second argument is word-sized and the first is not
description:
## Summary

When the first argument to `naturalAndNot` is larger than a `Word` and the second is `Word`-sized, `naturalAndNot` will truncate the result:

## Steps to reproduce

```haskell
module Main where

import Data.Bits
import GHC.Num.Natural

main :: IO ()
main =
  -- Ok, prints 16
  print $ naturalAndNot ((2 ^ 4) .|. (2 ^ 3)) (2 ^ 3)
  -- Ok, prints 680564733841876926926749214863536422912
  print $ naturalAndNot ((2 ^ 129) .|. (2 ^ 65)) (2 ^ 65)
  -- Ok, prints 16
  print $ naturalAndNot ((2 ^ 4) .|. (2 ^ 3)) ((2 ^ 65) .|. (2 ^ 3))
  -- Bug, prints 0
  print $ naturalAndNot ((2 ^ 65) .|. (2 ^ 3)) (2 ^ 3)
```

Please provide a set of concrete steps to reproduce the issue.

## Expected behavior

The result for the final `print` should be `2 ^ 65`

## Environment

* GHC version used: 9.12.2

Optional:

* Operating System: MacOS 13.7.6
* System Architecture: x86_64

title: Seg fault in messageBlackHole when using STM (GHC-9.10)
description:
We are encountering a segfault with the following backtrace in some code that is heavily using STM.

```
#0  messageBlackHole (cap=cap@entry=0x3d0b2ec0, msg=msg@entry=0x420ee47020) at rts/Messages.c:196
#1  0x000000000e6c36c3 in executeMessage (cap=cap@entry=0x3d0b2ec0, m=0x420ee47020) at rts/Messages.c:118
#2  0x000000000e6d322f in scheduleProcessInbox (pcap=<optimized out>) at rts/Schedule.c:1051
#3  scheduleFindWork (pcap=<optimized out>) at rts/Schedule.c:631
#4  schedule (initialCapability=initialCapability@entry=0x3d0b2ec0, task=task@entry=0x7f9b48083be0) at rts/Schedule.c:288
#5  0x000000000e6d434c in scheduleWorker (cap=cap@entry=0x3d0b2ec0, task=task@entry=0x7f9b48083be0) at rts/Schedule.c:2695
#6  0x000000000e6d899c in workerStart (task=0x7f9b48083be0) at rts/Task.c:444
#7  0x00007f9e33e60e63 in start_thread () from /nix/store/cg9s562sa33k78m63njfn1rw47dp9z0i-glibc-2.40-66/lib/libc.so.6
#8  0x00007f9e33ee4dbc in __clone3 () from /nix/store/cg9s562sa33k78m63njfn1rw47dp9z0i-glibc-2.40-66/lib/libc.so.6
```

The segfault seems to be caused by looking up an info pointer, which is just a random value. We've seen `0x15`, `0x300000000` and `0x200000000`.

Here is the assembly surrounding the issue:
```
   0xe6c3240 <messageBlackHole+96>:	mov    (%rax),%r12
   0xe6c3243 <messageBlackHole+99>:	mov    %r12,%rbp
   0xe6c3246 <messageBlackHole+102>:	and    $0xfffffffffffffff8,%rbp
=> 0xe6c324a <messageBlackHole+106>:	mov    0x0(%rbp),%rbx
   0xe6c324e <messageBlackHole+110>:	cmp    $0xe7139c0,%rbx
   0xe6c3255 <messageBlackHole+117>:	je     0xe6c3240 <messageBlackHole+96>
```
and the C code:
https://gitlab.haskell.org/ghc/ghc/-/blob/master/rts/Messages.c#L197
```c
    StgClosure *p;
    const StgInfoTable *info;
    do {
        p = UNTAG_CLOSURE(ACQUIRE_LOAD(&((StgInd*)bh)->indirectee));
        info = RELAXED_LOAD(&p->header.info);
    } while (info == &stg_IND_info);
```

dereferencing `info` is what is causing the fault.

Note that:
```
(gdb) p *(StgClosure*)((StgWord)((StgInd*)bh)->indirectee&(~7))
$16 = {header = {info = 0xe6bb718 <ghczmprim_GHCziTypes_ZC_con_info>}, payload = 0x4208045008}
(gdb) x/a $rax // I'm pretty sure rax is already meant to be untagged?
0x421f5c2030:	0x4208045002
(gdb) p *(StgClosure*)$rax
$17 = {header = {info = 0x4208045002}, payload = 0x421f5c2038}
(gdb) x/a $r12 // but r12 should be the value in rax
0x300000001:	0x0 // no wonder we get a segfault

```

It feels like there is some sort of race updating these values and we are left in some sort of inconsistent state.

In every single segfault, the black hole has looked like this:
```
(gdb) p *bh
$55 = {header = {info = 0xe713a00 <stg_BLACKHOLE_info>}, payload = 0x421f5c2030}
(gdb) p *(StgClosure*)0x4208045000
$58 = {header = {info = 0xe6bb718 <ghczmprim_GHCziTypes_ZC_con_info>}, payload = 0x4208045008}
```
That is to say we have a BLACKHOLE pointing at a cons cell.

### Context

The application makes heavy use of STM and C FFI. Initially we thought that some C code was causing havoc, but all of our segfaults occured in the same STM heavy code according to the Haskell stack of the TSO attached to this `MessageBlackHole`.

Increasing `-A` has seemed to mitigate the issue, so perhaps there is a link to GCs too. Previously the seg fault happened approximately every hour, after setting `-A64M`, it has only happened once since then.

We are using the regular copying collector.

We recently upgraded to GHC-9.10 and we have seen it with increasing frequency since. We aren't sure if this is causally linked to the bug, but it is suspicious.

GHC version: 9.10.2 from haskell.nix
OS: Linux-x86_64

title: scavenge_one doesn't handle WHITEHOLEs
description:
Currently `scavenge_one` fails to handle `WHITEHOLE`'d closures. It may encounter these while scavenging the mutable list via two paths:

1. when an MVAR is being marked concurrently as whiteholes are used to lock MVARs
2. when the object belongs to a chain of selectors being short-cutted.

This will result in errors of the form:
```
Badger.exe: internal error: scavenge_one: strange object 58
    (GHC version 9.8.2 for x86_64_unknown_mingw32)
    Please report this as a GHC bug:  https://www.haskell.org/ghc/reportabug
```

title: Foreign imports on Windows fail to use PC-relative references
description:
On Windows in the case of a program like,
```haskell
{-# LANGUAGE ForeignFunctionInterface #-}
module Hi where
import Foreign
foreign import ccall "&foo" foo :: Ptr Int
setIt :: IO ()
setIt = poke foo 423
```

We produce very simple code of the form:
```asm
.section .data
.align 8
.align 1
.globl Hi.foo_closure
Hi.foo_closure:
        .quad   GHC.Ptr.Ptr_static_info
        .quad   foo
```

However, this relies on Windows' pseudorelocation mechanism to relocate the `foo` reference which doesn't allow relocation in the full 48-bit address space of the process.

In the case of [gcc](https://godbolt.org/#g:!((g:!((g:!((h:codeEditor,i:(filename:'1',fontScale:14,fontUsePx:'0',j:1,lang:___c,selection:(endColumn:2,endLineNumber:4,positionColumn:2,positionLineNumber:4,selectionStartColumn:2,selectionStartLineNumber:4,startColumn:2,startLineNumber:4),source:'extern+int+foo%3B%0Aint+get()+%7B%0A++++foo+%3D+42%3B%0A%7D'),l:'5',n:'0',o:'C+source+%231',t:'0')),k:30.17840661045575,l:'4',n:'0',o:'',s:0,t:'0'),(g:!((h:compiler,i:(compiler:cmingw64_ucrt_gcc_1310,filters:(b:'0',binary:'1',binaryObject:'1',commentOnly:'0',debugCalls:'1',demangle:'0',directives:'0',execute:'1',intel:'1',libraryCode:'1',trim:'0',verboseDemangling:'0'),flagsViewOpen:'1',fontScale:14,fontUsePx:'0',j:1,lang:___c,libs:!(),options:'-O+-march%3Dskylake',overrides:!(),selection:(endColumn:1,endLineNumber:1,positionColumn:1,positionLineNumber:1,selectionStartColumn:1,selectionStartLineNumber:1,startColumn:1,startLineNumber:1),source:1),l:'5',n:'0',o:'+MinGW+gcc+13.1.0+(Editor+%231)',t:'0')),k:48.69021027091283,l:'4',n:'0',o:'',s:0,t:'0'),(g:!((h:output,i:(editorid:1,fontScale:14,fontUsePx:'0',j:1,wrap:'1'),l:'5',n:'0',o:'Output+of+MinGW+gcc+13.1.0+(Compiler+%231)',t:'0')),k:21.131383118631437,l:'4',n:'0',o:'',s:0,t:'0')),l:'2',n:'0',o:'',t:'0')),version:4) we instead see:

```asm
get:
  movq .refptr.foo(%rip), %rax
  movl $42, (%rax)
  ret
.refptr.foo:
  .quad foo
```

Which uses a PC relative reference to avoid assuming a small memory model.

title: llvm-ar 20 breaks runtime loader
description:
It appears that the behavior of `llvm-ar` has changed in version 20, resulting in loading failures of the form:

```
+ghc: loadArchive: GNU-variant filename offset 23 invalid (range [0..1012]) while reading filename from `C:/msys64/home/ben/ghc/_build/stage1/lib/ghc/ghc-internal-9.1300.0-inplace/libHSghc-internal-9.1300.0-inplace.a'
+panic! (the 'impossible' happened)
+  GHC version 9.13.20250623:
+       loadArchive "C://msys64//home//ben//ghc//_build//stage1//lib//ghc//ghc-internal-9.1300.0-inplace//libHSghc-internal-9.1300.0-inplace.a": failed
+
+Please report this as a GHC bug:  https://www.haskell.org/ghc/reportabug
```

It appears that the reason for this is https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/70233c61d6593a29cc2fe52044b9052d84c5f317/llvm/lib/Object/ArchiveWriter.cpp#L320, where `\0` is used as the file-name delimiter in the string table for COFF archives, unlike the usual `/\n` used on POSIX platforms.

It appears that this changed in https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/commit/4fcbf3842007569880fa916831efefda6b1bd032.

title: integerPowMod# and integerRecipMod# in GHC.Num.Integer return incorrect results
description:
## Summary

Two functions in `GHC.Num.Integer` produce incorrect results.

* `integerPowMod# 0 e m` returns `(# 0 | #)` for m \> 1 and e < 0. It should fail and return `(# | () #)`.
* `integerRecip# 0 1` fails and returns `(# | () #)`. It should succeed and return `(# 0 | #)`

## Steps to reproduce

```
$ cat Test.hs 

{-# LANGUAGE MagicHash #-} 
{-# LANGUAGE UnboxedSums #-}

module Test where

import GHC.Natural
import GHC.Num.Integer

-- Wrappers to make things easier in ghci
integerPowMod :: Integer -> Integer -> Natural -> Maybe Natural
integerPowMod b e m =
  case integerPowMod# b e m of
    (# n | #)  -> Just n
    (# | () #) -> Nothing

integerRecip :: Integer -> Natural -> Maybe Natural
integerRecip b m =
  case integerRecipMod# b m of
    (# n | #)  -> Just n
    (# | () #) -> Nothing

$ ghci Test.hs
GHCi, version 9.12.2: https://www.haskell.org/ghc/  :? for help
[1 of 1] Compiling Test             ( Test.hs, interpreted )
Ok, one module loaded.

ghci> integerPowMod 34 (-1) 17
Nothing  -- correct

ghci> integerPowMod 0 (-1) 17
Just 0   -- incorrect: should be Nothing.  Note that `34 ≡ 0 (mod 17)` but 34 and 0 give different results.

ghci> integerRecip 0 1
Nothing  -- incorrect: should (arguably) be Just 0
```

## Expected behaviour

### integerPowMod#

The exact behaviour of `integerPowMod#` and `integerRecipMod#` doesn't appear to be specified or documented anywhere, but when e\<0 I'd expect `integerPowMod# b e m` to return an integer b' with `b'^(-e)*b^(-e) ≡ 1 (mod m)` if `gcd b m == 1` (note that -e\>0, so the powers make sense) and to fail if `gcd b m /= 1`. When b=0, e\<0, and m\>1 it returns 0, which is certainly incorrect.

This happens because the [implementation of `integerPowMod#`](https://hackage-content.haskell.org/package/ghc-bignum-1.3/docs/src/GHC.Num.Integer.html#integerPowMod%23) begins

```
integerPowMod# :: Integer -> Integer -> Natural -> (# Natural | () #)
integerPowMod# !b !e !m
   | naturalIsZero m     = (# | () #)
   | naturalIsOne  m     = (# naturalZero | #)
   | integerIsZero e     = (# naturalOne  | #)
   | integerIsZero b     = (# naturalZero | #)
   | integerIsOne  b     = (# naturalOne  | #)
     -- when the exponent is negative, try to find the modular multiplicative
     -- inverse and use it instead
   | integerIsNegative e = case integerRecipMod# b m of ...
```

The `integerIsZero b` case returns 0 prematurely when b=0 and e\<0; it should be something like

```
   | integerIsZero b && e>0    = (# naturalZero | #)
```

and then either there should be a separate case for `integerIsZero b && e<0` or it should fall through to the `integerIsNegative` case and allow `integerRecipMod#` to produce an error.

### integerRecipMod#

The implementation of `integerRecipMod#` is

```
integerRecipMod#
   :: Integer
   -> Natural
   -> (# Natural | () #)
integerRecipMod# x m
   | integerIsZero x = (# | () #)
   | naturalIsZero m = (# | () #)
   | naturalIsOne  m = (# | () #)
   | True            = Backend.integer_recip_mod x m
```

so it always fails when m=1. I believe that it should return 0 in this case. I would expect `integerRecipMod# b m` to return an integer b' such that `b*b' ≡ 1 (mod m)`; in the case m=1, `0*0 = 0 ≡ 1 (mod m)`, as required. We're essentially working in the [zero ring](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zero_ring), which has a single element 0 with 0\*0=0, so 0 is a multiplicative identity element that happens to be called 0 instead of 1, and the multiplicative inverse of 0 is 0. Python's `pow` function and Sage's `power_mod` function both agree with this interpretation; note also that `integerPowMod# 0 (-1)  1` returns 0, so it agrees with my interpretation and disagrees with `integerRecipMod#`.

The expected behaviour can be obtained by changing the implementation to

```
integerRecipMod# x m
   | naturalIsZero m = (# | () #)
   | naturalIsOne  m = (# 0 | #)
   | integerIsZero x = (# | () #)
   | True            = Backend.integer_recip_mod x m
```

## Environment

GHC 9.2.12

title: HEAD regression (tcMatches: multiple arguments in EmptyCase)
description:
## Summary

GHC HEAD (as of commit 5977c6a1956f695fba55c07f9cde75443d775fc4) panics on a valid program that successfully compiles with GHC 9.12 or earlier.

## Steps to reproduce

Compile this program:

```hs
{-# LANGUAGE GHC2021 #-}
{-# LANGUAGE LambdaCase #-}
module Bug where

import Data.Void (Void)

f :: (forall a. Void -> a) -> (forall a. Void -> a)
f g = g

absurd :: Void -> a
absurd = f (\case)
-- absurd = \case   -- Doesn't panic
```

On GHC 9.12 or earlier this succeeds, but with GHC HEAD, this fails with:

```
$ ~/Software/ghc-9.13.20250411/bin/ghc Bug.hs
[1 of 1] Compiling Bug              ( Bug.hs, Bug.o )
<no location info>: error:
    panic! (the 'impossible' happened)
  GHC version 9.13.20250411:
        tcMatches: multiple arguments in EmptyCase
```

This regression was most likely introduced in commit cce869ea2439bb16c284ce7ed71a173d54a8c9ad (`Error message with EmptyCase and RequiredTypeArguments (#25004)`). cc @int-index 

## Expected behavior

The program continues to compile successfully using GHC HEAD.

## Environment

* GHC version used: HEAD (commit 5977c6a1956f695fba55c07f9cde75443d775fc4)

Optional:

* Operating System: Ubuntu 22.04
* System Architecture: x86-64

title: Compiled program segfault probably due to -fpolymorphic-specialisation
description:
## Summary

I'm guessing that the compiled program segfaults due to `-fpolymorphic-specialisation`, because the problem does not occur without the option and because some source tweaks that don't affect the computation remove the segfault, e.g., removing `r ~ Double` and inlining the `Double` or ceasing to call a function from another module but instead copying it and calling from the same module. This fragility is also what makes minimizing hard, but if that's helpful I can probably reduce this to a few modules (but rather not to a single module, see above).

The main file of the reproduction is https://github.com/Mikolaj/horde-ad/blob/9c0c490b2059fc5c08ffc3d0701ab7582536c7ae/test/simplified/TestMnistFCNNR.hs

I tried to debug the segfault itself, but the most I got so far it the following

```
cabal run testCommonLibrary --enable-optimization -w /home/mikolaj/r/ghc.HE/ghc/_build/stage1/bin/ghc --allow-newer --ghc-options="-fpolymorphic-specialisation -dcore-lint -dstg-lint -dcmm-lint -dtag-inference-checks"
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
gdb /home/mikolaj/r/horde-ad/dist-newstyle/build/x86_64-linux/ghc-9.13.20250130/horde-ad-0.1.0.0/x/testCommonLibrary/build/testCommonLibrary/testCommonLibrary
...
Thread 1 "testCommonLibra" received signal SIGSEGV, Segmentation fault.
0x0000000001195087 in ghczminternal_GHCziInternalziFloat_zdp1Floating_info ()
(gdb) bt
#0  0x0000000001195087 in ghczminternal_GHCziInternalziFloat_zdp1Floating_info ()
#1  0x0000000000000000 in ?? ()
(gdb) 
```

Before minimization I also got
```
(gdb) bt
#0  0x0000000009655597 in hordezmadzm0zi1zi0zi0zminplacezmhordezmadzmsimplified_HordeAdziCoreziTypes_zdp1RealFloatF_info ()
#1  0x0000000000000000 in ?? ()
(gdb)
```

## Steps to reproduce

```
git clone git@github.com:Mikolaj/horde-ad.git
cd horde-ad
git checkout repro-segfault-fpolymorphic-specialisation
curl https://ghc.gitlab.haskell.org/head.hackage/cabal.project > cabal.project.local
rm -rf ~/.cabal/packages/head.hackage.ghc.haskell.org
cabal update
cabal run testCommonLibrary --enable-optimization -w /home/mikolaj/r/ghc.HE/ghc/_build/stage1/bin/ghc --allow-newer --ghc-options="-fpolymorphic-specialisation -dcore-lint -dstg-lint -dcmm-lint -dtag-inference-checks"
```

## Expected behavior

Should not segfault.

## Environment

* GHC version used: 9.13.20250130

Optional:

* Operating System: Ubuntu
* System Architecture: AMD64

title: mod (i :: Word8) 7 sometimes results in values like 22 or 219
description:
```
git clone git@github.com:haskellari/binary-instances.git
cd binary-instances
git checkout ghc-9.12
cabal run binary-instances-test -w ghc-9.12.1 -- -p DayOfWeek
Configuration is affected by the following files:
- cabal.project
Roundtrip
  DayOfWeek: FAIL
    *** Failed! Exception: 'panic: get @DayOfWeek: (5,181)' (after 1 test):
    Friday
    Exception thrown while showing test case: 'panic: get @DayOfWeek: (5,181)'
    Use --quickcheck-replay="(SMGen 330261610994565626 12834802228157555811,0)" to reproduce.

1 out of 1 tests failed (0.00s)
```

The code of the test is running `get @DayOfWeek` which is

```haskell
    get = do
        i <- get
        return $ case mod (i :: Word8) 7 of
            0 -> Time.Sunday
            1 -> Time.Monday
            2 -> Time.Tuesday
            3 -> Time.Wednesday
            4 -> Time.Thursday
            5 -> Time.Friday
            6 -> Time.Saturday
            j -> error $ "panic: get @DayOfWeek: " ++ show (i, j)
```

I don't understand how the default case is reached. This happens every run.

It *never* happens with other GHC versions I tried, e.g. ghc-9.10.1 or 9.6.6.

title: Incorrect floating point values when using GHC API
description:
## Summary

On an M1 Mac, floating point literals are interpreted incorrectly by the GHC API when using `compileExpr`.  For instance (full code further below):
```
    let chck = "5.5626902089526504e-303 :: Double"
    v <- compileExpr chck
    print (unsafeCoerce v :: Double)
```
prints 
```
6.213763703753626e-303
```
On the Haskell discourse, other users with ARM processors have reported getting the _correct_ behavior (_not_ seeing this bug):
https://discourse.haskell.org/t/different-results-from-ghc-api-and-ghci/10988/8

## Steps to reproduce

On my (presumably any) M1 Mac, using GHC 9.6.6, 9.8.4, and 9.10.1, the below code:
```
module Main where

import GHC
import GHC.Paths
import Unsafe.Coerce
import Control.Monad.IO.Class


main :: IO ()
main = do
    runGhc (Just libdir) (do
        dyn_flags <- getSessionDynFlags
        _ <- setSessionDynFlags dyn_flags

        setContext [ IIDecl . simpleImportDecl . mkModuleName $ "Prelude"
                   , IIDecl . simpleImportDecl . mkModuleName $ "Unsafe.Coerce" ]
        
        wrong)

wrong :: Ghc ()
wrong = do
    let chck = "5.5626902089526504e-303 :: Double"
    v <- compileExpr chck

    liftIO $ do
        putStr "Direct: "
        print (5.5626902089526504e-303 :: Double)
        putStr "API:    "
        print (unsafeCoerce v :: Double)

    let chck2 = "5.56269020895265e-303 :: Double"
    v2 <- compileExpr chck2

    liftIO $ do
        putStr "Direct: "
        print (5.56269020895265e-303 :: Double)
        putStr "API:    "
        print (unsafeCoerce v2 :: Double)
```
outputs:
```
Direct: 5.5626902089526504e-303
API:    6.213763703753626e-303
Direct: 5.56269020895265e-303
API:    5.2321149654365974e-303
```
Notice that something about going through `compileExpr` turns `5.5626902089526504e-303` into `6.213763703753626e-303`, and `5.56269020895265e-303` into `5.2321149654365974e-303`.

## Additional testing:
This appears to have something to do with the floats being written as literals.  The below program, which uses hex encoding/encodeFloat, outputs correct results:
```
module Main where

import GHC
import GHC.Paths
import Unsafe.Coerce
import Control.Monad.IO.Class


main :: IO ()
main = do
    runGhc (Just libdir) (do
        dyn_flags <- getSessionDynFlags
        _ <- setSessionDynFlags dyn_flags

        setContext [ IIDecl . simpleImportDecl . mkModuleName $ "Prelude"
                   , IIDecl . simpleImportDecl . mkModuleName $ "Unsafe.Coerce" ]
        
        correct)

correct :: Ghc ()
correct = do
    let chck = "unsafeCoerce (0x012e848200000001 :: Integer) :: Double"
    v <- compileExpr chck

    liftIO $ do
        putStr "Direct: "
        print (unsafeCoerce (0x012e848200000001 :: Integer) :: Double)
        putStr "API:    "
        print (unsafeCoerce v :: Double)

    let chck2 = "encodeFloat 8589943181934593 (-1057) :: Double"
    v2 <- compileExpr chck2

    liftIO $ do
        putStr "Direct: "
        print (encodeFloat 8589943181934593 (-1057) :: Double)
        putStr "API:    "
        print (unsafeCoerce v2 :: Double)
```
